data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-25T01:34:10.794Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically regarding synchronous LINQ operations.

This is a knowledge question about EF Core and the Cosmos DB provider. Let me answer based on what I know.

In EF Core, the Azure Cosmos DB provider has been moving toward async-only APIs. In .NET 10 (EF Core 10), synchronous operations on the Cosmos DB provider were deprecated or required an opt-in flag. In .NET 11 (EF Core 11), the sync-over-async behavior would be fully removed.
 
Looking at the progression more carefully, the Cosmos DB provider has been gradually tightening its async requirements across versions. By .NET 11, the opt-in flag that allowed synchronous operations is gone entirely, so any sync LINQ calls like `ToList()`, `First()`, `Count()`, or `SaveChanges()` will throw an `InvalidOperationException` because the underlying Cosmos DB SDK doesn't support blocking calls.

The solution is straightforward: migrate all those synchronous operations to their async counterparts—`ToListAsync()`, `FirstAsync()`, `CountAsync()`, `SaveChangesAsync()`—throughout the codebase.
